text.NRSV: The Song of Songs, which is Solomon’s.
text.Rotherham: The Song of Songs, which pertaineth to Solomon.
text.KJV: 1:1 The song of songs, which is Solomons.
text.NABRE: Chapter 1 The Song of Songs, which is Solomon’s.
text.DRB: Let him kiss me with the kiss of his mouth: because thy breasts are better than wine.

text.NRSV: Let him kiss me with the kisses of his mouth! For your love is better than wine,
text.Rotherham: [SHE] Let him kiss me with the kisses of his mouth! [THEY] For better are thy caresses than wine:
text.KJV: 1:2 Let him kisse mee with the kisses of his mouth: for thy Loue is better then wine.
text.NABRE: The Woman Speaks of Her Lover W Let him kiss me with kisses of his mouth, for your love is better than wine,
text.DRB: smelling fragrantly of the best ointments. Oil poured out is thy name: therefore have young maids loved thee.

text.NRSV: your anointing oils are fragrant, your name is perfume poured out; therefore the maidens love you.
text.Rotherham: Like the fragrance of thy precious oils, Oil poured out, is thy name, For this cause, virgins love thee.
text.KJV: 1:3 Because of the sauour of thy good ointments, thy name is as ointment powred forth, therefore doe the virgins loue thee.
text.NABRE: better than the fragrance of your perfumes. Your name is a flowing perfume— therefore young women love you.
text.DRB: Draw me: we will run after thee in the odour of thine ointments. The king hath brought me into his cellars: we will rejoice & be glad in thee, mindful of thy breasts above wine: the righteous love thee.

text.NRSV: Draw me after you, let us make haste. The king has brought me into his chambers. We will exult and rejoice in you; we will extol your love more than wine; rightly do they love you.
text.Rotherham: [SHE] Draw me! [THEY] After thee, will we run! [SHE] The king, hath brought me, into his chambers. [THEY] We will exult and rejoice in thee, we will mention thy caresses, beyond wine, Sincerely they love thee.
text.KJV: 1:4 Draw me, we will runne after thee: the king hath brought me into his chambers: we will be glad and reioyce in thee, we wil remember thy loue more then wine: the vpright loue thee.
text.NABRE: Draw me after you! Let us run! The king has brought me to his bed chambers. Let us exult and rejoice in you; let us celebrate your love: it is beyond wine! Rightly do they love you!
text.DRB: I am black but beautiful, o ye daughters of Jerusalem, as the tabernacles of Cedar, as the skins of Salomon.

text.NRSV: I am black and beautiful, O daughters of Jerusalem, like the tents of Kedar, like the curtains of Solomon.
text.Rotherham: [SHE] Swarthy, I am but comely, ye daughters of Jerusalem. [THEY] Like the tents of Kedar, like the curtains of Solomon.
text.KJV: 1:5 I am blacke, but comely, (O ye daughters of Ierusalem) as the tents of Kedar, as the curtaines of Solomon.
text.NABRE: Love’s Boast W I am black and beautiful, Daughters of Jerusalem — Like the tents of Qedar, like the curtains of Solomon.
text.DRB: Do not consider me that I am brown, because the sun hath altered my colour: the sons of my mother have fought against me, they have made me a keeper in the vineyards: my vineyard I have not kept.

text.NRSV: Do not gaze at me because I am dark, because the sun has gazed on me. My mother’s sons were angry with me; they made me keeper of the vineyards, but my own vineyard I have not kept!
text.Rotherham: [SHE] Do not look on me, because, I, am so swarthy, because the sun hath scorched me,—My mother’s sons, were angry with me, they set me to keep the vineyards, Mine own vineyard, have I not kept. …
text.KJV: 1:6 Looke not vpon me because I am blacke, because the Sunne hath looked vpon me: my mothers children were angry with me, they made me the keeper of the vineyards, but mine owne vineyard haue I not kept.
text.NABRE: Do not stare at me because I am so black, because the sun has burned me. The sons of my mother were angry with me; they charged me with the care of the vineyards: my own vineyard I did not take care of.
text.DRB: Shew me o thou, whom my soul loveth, where thou feedest, where thou liest in the midday, lest I begin to wander after the flocks of thy companions.

text.NRSV: Tell me, you whom my soul loves, where you pasture your flock, where you make it lie down at noon; for why should I be like one who is veiled beside the flocks of your companions?
text.Rotherham: Tell me, thou loved of my soul! Where wilt thou pasture thy flock? Where wilt thou let them recline at noon? For why should I be as one that wrappeth a veil about her, by the flocks of thy companions?
text.KJV: 1:7 Tell me, (O thou whom my soule loueth) where thou feedest, where thou makest thy flocke to rest at noone: for why should I be as one that turneth aside by the flockes of thy companions?
text.NABRE: Love’s Inquiry W Tell me, you whom my soul loves, where you shepherd, where you give rest at midday. Why should I be like one wandering after the flocks of your companions?
text.DRB: If thou know not thyself, o most fairest among women, go forth, and follow after the steps of the flocks, and feed thy kids beside the tabernacles of the pastors.

text.NRSV: If you do not know, O fairest among women, follow the track of the flock, and pasture your kids beside the shepherds’ tents.
text.Rotherham: [HE] If thou know not of thyself, most beautiful among women! get thee forth in the footsteps of the flock, and pasture thy kids by the huts of the shepherds. …
text.KJV: 1:8 ¶ If thou know not (O thou fairest among women) goe thy way forth by the footsteps of the flocke, and feede thy kiddes beside the shepheards tents.
text.NABRE: M If you do not know, most beautiful among women, Follow the tracks of the flock and pasture your lambs near the shepherds’ tents.
text.DRB: To my company of horsemen, in the chariots of Pharao, have I likened thee, o my love.

text.NRSV: I compare you, my love, to a mare among Pharaoh’s chariots.
text.Rotherham: To a mare of mine, in the chariots of Pharaoh, have I likened thee, my fair one!
text.KJV: 1:9 I haue compared thee, O my loue, to a company of horses in Pharaohs chariots.
text.NABRE: Love’s Vision M To a mare among Pharaoh’s chariotry I compare you, my friend:
text.DRB: Thy cheeks are beautiful as the turtle-doves, thy neck as jewels.

text.NRSV: Your cheeks are comely with ornaments, your neck with strings of jewels.
text.Rotherham: Comely are thy cheeks, with bead-rows, thy neck, with strings of gems.
text.KJV: 1:10 Thy cheekes are comely with rowes of iewels, thy necke with chaines of golde.
text.NABRE: Your cheeks lovely in pendants, your neck in jewels.
text.DRB: We will make thee chains of gold, enamelled with silver.

text.NRSV: We will make you ornaments of gold, studded with silver.
text.Rotherham: [THEY] Rows of golden ornaments, will we make thee, with studs of silver.
text.KJV: 1:11 Wee will make thee borders of golde, with studdes of siluer.
text.NABRE: We will make pendants of gold for you, and ornaments of silver.
text.DRB: Whilst the king was at his repose, my spikenard gave the odour thereof.

text.NRSV: While the king was at his repose, my nard gave forth its fragrance.
text.Rotherham: [SHE] By the time the king is in his circle, my nard, will have given out its fragrance:
text.KJV: 1:12 ¶ While the king sitteth at his table, my spikenard sendeth foorth the smell thereof.
text.NABRE: How Near Is Love! W While the king was upon his couch, my spikenard gave forth its fragrance.
text.DRB: Abundle of myrrh my beloved is to me, he shall abide between my breasts.

text.NRSV: My beloved is to me a bag of myrrh that lies between my breasts.
text.Rotherham: A bag of myrrh, is my beloved to me, between my breasts, shall it tarry the night!
text.KJV: 1:13 A bundle of myrrhe is my welbeloued vnto me; he shall lie all night betwixt my breasts.
text.NABRE: My lover is to me a sachet of myrrh; between my breasts he lies.
text.DRB: Acluster of cypress my love is to me, in the vineyards of Engaddi.

text.NRSV: My beloved is to me a cluster of henna blossoms in the vineyards of En-gedi.
text.Rotherham: A cluster of henna, is my beloved to me, in the vineyards of En-gedi.
text.KJV: 1:14 My beloued is vnto me, as a cluster of Camphire in the vineyards of Engedi.
text.NABRE: My lover is to me a cluster of henna from the vineyards of En-gedi.
text.DRB: Behold thou art fair, o my love, behold thou art fair, thine eyes are as of doves'.

text.NRSV: Ah, you are beautiful, my love; ah, you are beautiful; your eyes are doves.
text.Rotherham: [HE] Lo! thou art beautiful my fair one, lo! thou art beautiful, Thine eyes, [are] doves!
text.KJV: 1:15 Behold, thou art faire, my loue: behold, thou art faire, thou hast doues eyes.
text.NABRE: M How beautiful you are, my friend, how beautiful! your eyes are doves!
text.DRB: Behold thou art fair my beloved, & comely: our little bed is flourishing.

text.NRSV: Ah, you are beautiful, my beloved, truly lovely. Our couch is green;
text.Rotherham: [SHE] Lo! thou art beautiful, my beloved, Yea delightful! [BOTH] Yea! our couch, is covered with leaves:
text.KJV: 1:16 Behold, thou art faire, my beloued; yea pleasant: also our bedde is greene.
text.NABRE: W How beautiful you are, my lover— handsome indeed! Verdant indeed is our couch;
text.DRB: The beams of our houses are of cedar, our rafters of cypress trees.

text.NRSV: the beams of our house are cedar, our rafters are pine.
text.Rotherham: The beams of our house, are cedars, Our fretted ceiling, is cypress-trees.
text.KJV: 1:17 The beames of our house are Cedar, and our rafters of firre.
text.NABRE: the beams of our house are cedars, our rafters, cypresses.

text.NRSV: I am a rose of Sharon, a lily of the valleys.
text.Rotherham: [SHE] I am The meadow-saffron of Sharon, The lily of the valleys.
text.KJV: 2:1 I Am the rose of Sharon, and the lillie of the valleys.
text.NABRE: Chapter 2 W I am a flower of Sharon, a lily of the valleys.
text.DRB: I am the flower of the field, and the lily of the valleys.

text.NRSV: As a lily among brambles, so is my love among maidens.
text.Rotherham: [HE] As a lily among thorns, So, is my fair one, among the daughters!
text.KJV: 2:2 As the lillie among thornes, so is my loue among the daughters.
text.NABRE: M Like a lily among thorns, so is my friend among women.
text.DRB: As the lily among the thorns, so is my love among the daughters.

text.NRSV: As an apple tree among the trees of the wood, so is my beloved among young men. With great delight I sat in his shadow, and his fruit was sweet to my taste.
text.Rotherham: [SHE] As an apple-tree among the trees of the forest, So, is my beloved, among the sons: In his shade, I greatly delighted and sat down, And, his fruit, was sweet to my taste.
text.KJV: 2:3 As the apple tree among the trees of the wood, so is my beloued among the sonnes. I sate downe vnder his shadow with great delight, and his fruit was sweete to my taste.
text.NABRE: W Like an apple tree among the trees of the woods, so is my lover among men. In his shadow I delight to sit, and his fruit is sweet to my taste.
text.DRB: As the apple-tree among trees of the woods, so is my beloved among the sons. Under his shadow, whom I desired, I sat: and his fruit was sweet unto my throat.

text.NRSV: He brought me to the banqueting house, and his banner over me was love.
text.Rotherham: He hath brought me into the house of wine, and, his banner over me, is love.
text.KJV: 2:4 Hee brought me to the banketting house, and his banner ouer mee, was loue.
text.NABRE: He brought me to the banquet hall and his glance at me signaled love.
text.DRB: He brought me into the wine cellar, he hath ordered in me charity.

text.NRSV: Sustain me with raisins, refresh me with apples; for I am faint with love.
text.Rotherham: Sustain me with raisin-cakes, refresh me with apples,—for sick with love, I am.
text.KJV: 2:5 Stay me with flagons, comfort me with apples, for I am sicke of loue.
text.NABRE: Strengthen me with raisin cakes, refresh me with apples, for I am sick with love.
text.DRB: Stay me up with flowers, compass me about with apples: because I languish with love.

text.NRSV: O that his left hand were under my head, and that his right hand embraced me!
text.Rotherham: His left hand under my head, then, his right hand, embraceth me!
text.KJV: 2:6 His left hand is vnder my head, and his right hand doeth imbrace me.
text.NABRE: His left hand is under my head and his right arm embraces me.
text.DRB: His left hand under my head, and his right hand shall embrace me.

text.NRSV: I adjure you, O daughters of Jerusalem, by the gazelles or the wild does: do not stir up or awaken love until it is ready.
text.Rotherham: [HE] I adjure you, ye daughters of Jerusalem, by the gazelles or by the hinds of the field,—That ye wake not, nor arouse, the dear love until she please! [****]
text.KJV: 2:7 I charge you, O ye daughters of Ierusalem, by the Roes, and by the hindes of the field, that ye stirre not vp, nor awake my loue, till she please.
text.NABRE: I adjure you, Daughters of Jerusalem, by the gazelles and the does of the field, Do not awaken, or stir up love until it is ready.
text.DRB: I adjure you o daughters of Jerusalem, by the roes, and the harts of the fields that you raise not, nor make the beloved to awake, until herself will.

text.NRSV: The voice of my beloved! Look, he comes, leaping upon the mountains, bounding over the hills.
text.Rotherham: [SHE] The voice of my beloved! Lo! here he cometh,—leaping over the mountains, skipping over the hills.
text.KJV: 2:8 ¶ The voice of my beloued! behold! hee commeth leaping vpon the mountaines, skipping vpon the hils.
text.NABRE: Her Lover’s Visit Remembered W The sound of my lover! here he comes springing across the mountains, leaping across the hills.
text.DRB: The voice of my beloved, behold he cometh leaping in the mountains, leaping over the little hills:

text.NRSV: My beloved is like a gazelle or a young stag. Look, there he stands behind our wall, gazing in at the windows, looking through the lattice.
text.Rotherham: Resembleth, my beloved, a gazelle, or a young stag,—Lo! here he is, standing behind our wall, looking in at the windows, peeping in at the lattice.
text.KJV: 2:9 My beloued is like a Roe, or a yong Hart: behold, he standeth behind our wall, he looketh foorth at the windowe, shewing himselfe through the lattesse.
text.NABRE: My lover is like a gazelle or a young stag. See! He is standing behind our wall, gazing through the windows, peering through the lattices.
text.DRB: my beloved is like unto a roe, and to a fawn of harts. Behold he standeth behind our wall, looking through the window, looking forth by the grates.

text.NRSV: My beloved speaks and says to me: “Arise, my love, my fair one, and come away;
text.Rotherham: Responded my beloved, and said to me,—Rise up! my fair—my beautiful—one, and come away,
text.KJV: 2:10 My beloued spake, and said vnto me, Rise vp, my Loue, my faire one, and come away.
text.NABRE: My lover speaks and says to me, M “Arise, my friend, my beautiful one, and come!
text.DRB: Behold my beloved speaketh to me: Arise, make haste my love, my dove, my beautiful one, and come.

text.NRSV: for now the winter is past, the rain is over and gone.
text.Rotherham: For lo, the winter, is past,—the rain, is over, [and] gone;
text.KJV: 2:11 For loe, the winter is past, the raine is ouer, and gone.
text.NABRE: For see, the winter is past, the rains are over and gone.
text.DRB: For winter is now past, the rain is gone, and departed.

text.NRSV: The flowers appear on the earth; the time of singing has come, and the voice of the turtle-dove is heard in our land.
text.Rotherham: The flowers, have appeared in the earth, the time of the spring-song, hath come,—and, the voice of the turtle, is heard in our land;
text.KJV: 2:12 The flowers appeare on the earth, the time of the singing of birds is come, and the voice of the turtle is heard in our land.
text.NABRE: The flowers appear on the earth, the time of pruning the vines has come, and the song of the turtledove is heard in our land.
text.DRB: The flowers have appeared in our land, the time of pruning is come: the voice of the turtle-dove is heard in our land:

text.NRSV: The fig tree puts forth its figs, and the vines are in blossom; they give forth fragrance. Arise, my love, my fair one, and come away.
text.Rotherham: The fig-tree, hath spiced her green figs, and, the vines—all blossom, yield fragrance,—Rise up! my fair—my beautiful—one, and come away!
text.KJV: 2:13 The fig tree putteth foorth her greene figs, and the vines with the tender grape giue a good smell. Arise, my loue, my faire one, and come away.
text.NABRE: The fig tree puts forth its figs, and the vines, in bloom, give forth fragrance. Arise, my friend, my beautiful one, and come!
text.DRB: the fig-tree hath brought forth her green figs: the flourishing vineyards have given their savour. Arise my love, my beautiful one, & come.

text.NRSV: O my dove, in the clefts of the rock, in the covert of the cliff, let me see your face, let me hear your voice; for your voice is sweet, and your face is lovely.
text.Rotherham: [HE] O my dove! In the retreats of the crag, in the hiding-place of the terrace, Let me see thy form, Let me hear thy voice,—For, thy voice, is sweet, and, thy form, comely.
text.KJV: 2:14 ¶ O my doue! that art in the clefts of the rocke, in the secret places of the staires: let me see thy countenance, let me heare thy voice, for sweet is thy voice, and thy countenance is comely.
text.NABRE: My dove in the clefts of the rock, in the secret recesses of the cliff, Let me see your face, let me hear your voice, For your voice is sweet, and your face is lovely.”
text.DRB: My dove in the holes of the rock, in the hollow places of the wall, shew me thy face, let thy voice sound in mine ears: for thy voice is sweet, and thy face comely.

text.NRSV: Catch us the foxes, the little foxes, that rob the vineyards— for our vineyards are in blossom.”
text.Rotherham: [BOTH] Take ye for us, the foxes, the little foxes that are spoiling the vines,—and, our vines, are all blossom!
text.KJV: 2:15 Take vs the foxes, the litle foxes, that spoile the vines: for our vines haue tender grapes.
text.NABRE: W Catch us the foxes, the little foxes that damage the vineyards; for our vineyards are in bloom!
text.DRB: Catch us the little foxes, that destroy the vineyards: for our vineyard hath flourished.

text.NRSV: My beloved is mine and I am his; he pastures his flock among the lilies.
text.Rotherham: [SHE] My beloved, is, mine, and, I, am, his, he that pastureth among lilies!
text.KJV: 2:16 ¶ My beloued is mine, and I am his: he feedeth among the lillies.
text.NABRE: My lover belongs to me and I to him; he feeds among the lilies.
text.DRB: My beloved to me, and I to him, who feedeth among the lilies,

text.NRSV: Until the day breathes and the shadows flee, turn, my beloved, be like a gazelle or a young stag on the cleft mountains.
text.Rotherham: Until the day, breathe, and the shadows, be lengthened, Again, liken thyself, my beloved, to a gazelle, or to a young stag, upon the cleft mountains. [****]
text.KJV: 2:17 Untill the day breake, and the shadowes flee away: turne my beloued and be thou like a Roe, or a yong Hart, vpon the mountaines of Bether.
text.NABRE: Until the day grows cool and the shadows flee, roam, my lover, Like a gazelle or a young stag upon the rugged mountains.
text.DRB: till the day break, and the shadows decline. Return: be like, my beloved, to a roe, and to the fawn of harts upon the mountains of Bether.

text.NRSV: Upon my bed at night I sought him whom my soul loves; I sought him, but found him not; I called him, but he gave no answer.
text.Rotherham: [SHE] Upon my couch, in the night-time, sought I the beloved of my soul,—I sought him, but found him not.
text.KJV: 3:1 By night on my bed I sought him whome my soule loueth. I sought him, but I found him not.
text.NABRE: Chapter 3 Loss and Discovery W On my bed at night I sought him whom my soul loves— I sought him but I did not find him.
text.DRB: In my little bed in the nights I have sought him whom my soul loveth, I have sought him, and have not found.

text.NRSV: “I will rise now and go about the city, in the streets and in the squares; I will seek him whom my soul loves.” I sought him, but found him not.
text.Rotherham: Come! I must arise, and go about in the city, In the paths and in the broadways, I must seek the beloved of my soul,—I sought him, but found him not.
text.KJV: 3:2 I will rise now, and goe about the citie in the streets, and in the broad wayes I will seeke him whom my soule loueth: I sought him, but I found him not.
text.NABRE: “Let me rise then and go about the city, through the streets and squares; Let me seek him whom my soul loves.” I sought him but I did not find him.
text.DRB: I will rise, and will go about the city: by the streets and high ways I will seek him whom my soul loveth: I have sought him, and have not found.

text.NRSV: The sentinels found me as they went about in the city. “Have you seen him whom my soul loves?”
text.Rotherham: The watchmen that go round in the city, found me, The beloved of my soul, have ye seen?
text.KJV: 3:3 The watchmen that goe about the citie, found me: to whom I said, Saw ye him whom my soule loueth?
text.NABRE: The watchmen found me, as they made their rounds in the city: “Him whom my soul loves—have you seen him?”
text.DRB: The watchmen which keep the city found me: Have you seen him, whom my soul loveth?

text.NRSV: Scarcely had I passed them when I found him whom my soul loves. I held him, and would not let him go until I brought him into my mother’s house, and into the chamber of her that conceived me.
text.Rotherham: Scarcely had I passed from them, when I found the beloved of my soul,—I caught him, and would not let him go, until that I had brought him into the house of my mother, and into the chamber of her that conceived me.
text.KJV: 3:4 It was but a litle that I passed from them, but I found him whome my soule loueth: I helde him, and would not let him goe, vntill I had brought him into my mothers house, and into the chamber of her that conceiued me.
text.NABRE: Hardly had I left them when I found him whom my soul loves. I held him and would not let him go until I had brought him to my mother’s house, to the chamber of her who conceived me.
text.DRB: When I had a little passed by them, I found him whom my soul loveth: I held him: neither will I let him go, till I bring him into my mother's house, and into the chamber of her that bare me.

text.NRSV: I adjure you, O daughters of Jerusalem, by the gazelles or the wild does: do not stir up or awaken love until it is ready.
text.Rotherham: [HE] I adjure you ye, daughters of Jerusalem, by the gazelles, or by the hinds of the field,—That ye wake not, nor arouse, the dear love until she please. [****]
text.KJV: 3:5 I charge you, O ye daughters of Ierusalem, by the Roes and by the Hindes of the field, that ye stirre not vp, nor awake my loue, till he please.
text.NABRE: I adjure you, Daughters of Jerusalem, by the gazelles and the does of the field, Do not awaken or stir up love until it is ready.
text.DRB: I adjure you, o daughters of Jerusalem by the roes, and the harts of the fields, that you raise not up, nor make the beloved to awake, till herself will.

text.NRSV: What is that coming up from the wilderness, like a column of smoke, perfumed with myrrh and frankincense, with all the fragrant powders of the merchant?
text.Rotherham: [THEY] Who is this, coming up out of the wilderness, like pillars of smoke,—with perfume of myrrh, and frankincense, besides all the aromatic powder of the merchant?
text.KJV: 3:6 ¶ Who is this that commeth out of the wildernes like pillars of smoke, perfumed with myrrhe and frankincense, with all powders of the merchant?
text.NABRE: Solomon’s Wedding Procession D Who is this coming up from the desert, like columns of smoke Perfumed with myrrh and frankincense, with all kinds of exotic powders?
text.DRB: What is she, that ascendeth by the desert, as a little rod of smoke of the aromatical spices of myrrh and frankincense, & of all powder of the apothecary?

text.NRSV: Look, it is the litter of Solomon! Around it are sixty mighty men of the mighty men of Israel,
text.Rotherham: Lo! his couch, ’tis Solomon’s own, Threescore heroes, around it,—of the heroes of Israel:
text.KJV: 3:7 Behold his bed, which is Solomons: threescore valiant men are about it, of the valiant of Israel:
text.NABRE: See! it is the litter of Solomon; sixty valiant men surround it, of the valiant men of Israel:
text.DRB: Behold threescore valiants of the most valiant of Israel, compass the little bed of Salomon:

text.NRSV: all equipped with swords and expert in war, each with his sword at his thigh because of alarms by night.
text.Rotherham: All of them, grasping the sword, trained for war,—every man, with his sword upon his thigh, because of dread, in the night-time.
text.KJV: 3:8 They all hold swords, being expert in warre: Euery man hath his sword vpon his thigh, because of feare in the night.
text.NABRE: All of them expert with the sword, skilled in battle, Each with his sword at his side against the terrors of the night.
text.DRB: all holding swords, and most cunning to battles: every man's sword upon his thigh for fears by night.

text.NRSV: King Solomon made himself a palanquin from the wood of Lebanon.
text.Rotherham: A palanquin, King Solomon made himself, of the trees of Lebanon:
text.KJV: 3:9 King Solomon made himselfe a charet of the wood of Lebanon.
text.NABRE: King Solomon made himself an enclosed litter of wood from Lebanon.
text.DRB: King Salomon hath made him a portable throne of the wood of Libanus:

text.NRSV: He made its posts of silver, its back of gold, its seat of purple; its interior was inlaid with love by the daughters of Jerusalem.
text.Rotherham: The supports thereof, made he of silver, the couch thereof, of gold, the seat thereof, of purple,—the midst thereof, hath an inlay of love from the daughters of Jerusalem.
text.KJV: 3:10 He made the pillars thereof of siluer, the bottome thereof of gold, the couering of it, of purple; the midst thereof being paued with loue, for the daughters of Ierusalem.
text.NABRE: He made its columns of silver, its roof of gold, Its seat of purple cloth, its interior lovingly fitted. Daughters of Jerusalem,
text.DRB: the pillars thereof he hath made of silver, the seat of gold, the going up of purple: the midst he hath paved with charity for the daughters of Jerusalem.

text.NRSV: Come out, O daughters of Zion, and look at King Solomon, at the crown with which his mother crowned him on the day of his wedding, on the day of the gladness of his heart.
text.Rotherham: Go forth and gaze, ye daughters of Zion, upon King Solomon,—wearing the crown, wherewith his mother, crowned him, in the day of his marriage, and in the day of his heart gladness.
text.KJV: 3:11 Goe foorth, O yee daughters of Zion, and behold king Solomon with the Crowne wherewith his mother crowned him in the day of his espousals, and in the day of the gladnesse of his heart.
text.NABRE: go out and look upon King Solomon In the crown with which his mother has crowned him on the day of his marriage, on the day of the joy of his heart.
text.DRB: Go forth ye daughters of Sion, and see king Salomon in the diadem, wherewith his mother hath crowned him in the day of his despousing, and in the day of the joy of his heart.

text.NRSV: How beautiful you are, my love, how very beautiful! Your eyes are doves behind your veil. Your hair is like a flock of goats, moving down the slopes of Gilead.
text.Rotherham: [HE] Lo! thou art beautiful, my fair one, Lo! thou art beautiful, Thine eyes, are doves, from behind thy veil,—Thy hair, is like a flock of goats, which are reclining on the sides of Mount Gilead:
text.KJV: 4:1 Behold, thou art faire, my loue, behold thou art faire, thou hast doues eyes within thy lockes: thy haire is as a flocke of goats, that appeare from mount Gilead.
text.NABRE: Chapter 4 The Beauty of the Woman M, How beautiful you are, my friend, how beautiful you are! Your eyes are doves behind your veil. Your hair is like a flock of goats streaming down Mount Gilead.
text.DRB: How beautiful art thou my love, how beautiful art thou! Thine eyes as it were of doves', besides that which lieth hid within. Thy ears as the flocks of goats, which have come up from mount Galaad.

text.NRSV: Your teeth are like a flock of shorn ewes that have come up from the washing, all of which bear twins, and not one among them is bereaved.
text.Rotherham: Thy teeth, are like a flock, evenly grown, which have come up from the washing-place,—whereof, all of them, are twin-bearers, and bereaved, is none among them:
text.KJV: 4:2 Thy teeth are like a flocke of sheepe that are euen shorne, which came vp from the washing: whereof euery one beare twinnes, and none is barren among them.
text.NABRE: Your teeth are like a flock of ewes to be shorn, that come up from the washing, All of them big with twins, none of them barren.
text.DRB: Thy teeth as flocks of them that are shorn, which have come up from the lavatory, all with twins, and there is no barren among them.

text.NRSV: Your lips are like a crimson thread, and your mouth is lovely. Your cheeks are like halves of a pomegranate behind your veil.
text.Rotherham: Like a cord of crimson, are thy lips, and, thy mouth, is lovely,—Like a slice of pomegranate, are thy temples, behind thy veil:
text.KJV: 4:3 Thy lips are like a threed of scarlet, and thy speach is comely: thy temples are like a piece of a pomegranate within thy lockes.
text.NABRE: Like a scarlet strand, your lips, and your mouth—lovely! Like pomegranate halves, your cheeks behind your veil.
text.DRB: Thy lips as a scarlet lace: and thy speech sweet. As a piece of a pomegranate, so are also thy cheeks, besides that which lieth hid within.

text.NRSV: Your neck is like the tower of David, built in courses; on it hang a thousand bucklers, all of them shields of warriors.
text.Rotherham: Like the tower of David, is thy neck, built for war,—A thousand shields, hung thereon, all, equipment of heroes:
text.KJV: 4:4 Thy necke is like the tower of Dauid builded for an armorie, whereon there hang a thousand bucklers, all shields of mightie men.
text.NABRE: Like a tower of David, your neck, built in courses, A thousand shields hanging upon it, all the armor of warriors.
text.DRB: Thy neck is as the tower of David, which is built with bulwarks: a thousand targets hang on it, all the armour of the valiants.

text.NRSV: Your two breasts are like two fawns, twins of a gazelle, that feed among the lilies.
text.Rotherham: Thy two breasts, are like two young roes, twins of a gazelle,—which pasture among lilies.
text.KJV: 4:5 Thy two breasts, are like two yong Roes, that are twinnes, which feed among the lillies.
text.NABRE: Your breasts are like two fawns, twins of a gazelle feeding among the lilies.
text.DRB: Thy two breasts as two fawns the twins of a row, which feed among the lilies,

text.NRSV: Until the day breathes and the shadows flee, I will hasten to the mountain of myrrh and the hill of frankincense.
text.Rotherham: Until the day, breathe, and the shadows, be lengthened, I will get me unto the mountain of myrrh, and unto the hill of frankincense.
text.KJV: 4:6 Untill the day breake, and the shadowes flee away, I will get mee to the mountaines of myrrhe, and to the hill of frankincense.
text.NABRE: Until the day grows cool and the shadows flee, I shall go to the mountain of myrrh, to the hill of frankincense.
text.DRB: till the day aspire, and the shadows decline. I will go to the mount of myrrh, and to the little hill of frankincense.

text.NRSV: You are altogether beautiful, my love; there is no flaw in you.
text.Rotherham: Thou art, all over, beautiful, my fair one, and, blemish, is there none in thee.
text.KJV: 4:7 Thou art all faire, my loue, there is no spot in thee.
text.NABRE: You are beautiful in every way, my friend, there is no flaw in you!
text.DRB: Thou art all fair o my love, and there is not a spot in thee.

text.NRSV: Come with me from Lebanon, my bride; come with me from Lebanon. Depart from the peak of Amana, from the peak of Senir and Hermon, from the dens of lions, from the mountains of leopards.
text.Rotherham: With me, from Lebanon, O bride, with me, from Lebanon, shalt thou enter,—Thou shalt look round from the top of Amana, from the top of Senir, and Hermon, from the dens of lions, from the mountains of leopards.
text.KJV: 4:8 ¶ Come with me from Lebanon (my spouse,) with me from Lebanon: looke from the top of Amana, from the top of Shenir and Hermon, from the Lions dennes, from the mountaines of the Leopards.
text.NABRE: With me from Lebanon, my bride! With me from Lebanon, come! Descend from the peak of Amana, from the peak of Senir and Hermon, From the lairs of lions, from the leopards’ heights.
text.DRB: Come from Libanus my spouse, come from Libanus, come: thou shalt be crowned from the head of Amana, from the top of Sanir & Hermon, from the dens of lions, from the mountains of leopards.

text.NRSV: You have ravished my heart, my sister, my bride, you have ravished my heart with a glance of your eyes, with one jewel of your necklace.
text.Rotherham: Thou hast encouraged me, my sister, bride,—thou hast encouraged me, with one [glance] of thine eyes, with one ornament of thy neck.
text.KJV: 4:9 Thou hast rauished my heart, my sister, my spouse; thou hast rauished my heart, with one of thine eyes, with one chaine of thy necke.
text.NABRE: You have ravished my heart, my sister, my bride; you have ravished my heart with one glance of your eyes, with one bead of your necklace.
text.DRB: Thou hast wounded my heart, my sister spouse, thou hast wounded my heart in one of thine eyes, and in one hair of thy neck.

text.NRSV: How sweet is your love, my sister, my bride! how much better is your love than wine, and the fragrance of your oils than any spice!
text.Rotherham: How beautiful are thy caresses, my sister, bride,—how much more delightful thy caresses, than wine, and the fragrance of thine oils, than all spices:
text.KJV: 4:10 How faire is thy loue, my sister, my spouse! how much better is thy loue then wine! and the smell of thine oyntments then all spices!
text.NABRE: How beautiful is your love, my sister, my bride, How much better is your love than wine, and the fragrance of your perfumes than any spice!
text.DRB: How beautiful are thy breasts my sister spouse! Thy breasts are more beautiful than wine, and the odour of thine ointments above all aromatical spices.

text.NRSV: Your lips distill nectar, my bride; honey and milk are under your tongue; the scent of your garments is like the scent of Lebanon.
text.Rotherham: With sweetness, thy lips do drip, O bride,—Honey and milk, are under thy tongue, and, the fragrance of thy garments, is like the fragrance of Lebanon.
text.KJV: 4:11 Thy lips, O my spouse! drop as the hony combe: hony and milke are vnder thy tongue, and the smell of thy garments is like the smell of Lebanon.
text.NABRE: Your lips drip honey, my bride, honey and milk are under your tongue; And the fragrance of your garments is like the fragrance of Lebanon.
text.DRB: Thy lips my spouse are as an honey-comb distilling, honey and milk are under thy tongue: and the odour of thy garments as the odour of frankincense.

text.NRSV: A garden locked is my sister, my bride, a garden locked, a fountain sealed.
text.Rotherham: A garden barred, is my sister, bride,—a spring barred, a fountain sealed:
text.KJV: 4:12 A garden inclosed is my sister, my spouse: a spring shut vp, a fountaine sealed.
text.NABRE: The Lover’s Garden M A garden enclosed, my sister, my bride, a garden enclosed, a fountain sealed!
text.DRB: My sister spouse is a garden inclosed, a garden inclosed, a fountain sealed up.

text.NRSV: Your channel is an orchard of pomegranates with all choicest fruits, henna with nard,
text.Rotherham: Thy buddings forth, are a paradise of pomegranates, with precious fruits,—henna bushes, with nard blossoms:
text.KJV: 4:13 Thy plants are an orchard of pomegranates, with pleasant fruits, Camphire, with Spikenaed,
text.NABRE: Your branches are a grove of pomegranates, with fruits of choicest yield: Henna with spikenard,
text.DRB: Thy offsprings a paradise of pomegranates with orchard fruits. Cypress with spikenard,

text.NRSV: nard and saffron, calamus and cinnamon, with all trees of frankincense, myrrh and aloes, with all chief spices—
text.Rotherham: Nard and saffron, sweet cane and cinnamon, with all woods of frankincense,—myrrh and aloes, with all the chiefs of spices:
text.KJV: 4:14 Spikenard and Saffron, Calamus and Cynamom, with all trees of Frankincense, Mirrhe and Aloes, with all the chiefe spices.
text.NABRE: spikenard and saffron, Sweet cane and cinnamon, with all kinds of frankincense; Myrrh and aloes, with all the finest spices;
text.DRB: spikenard, and saffron, sweet cane and cinnamon, with all the trees of Libanus, myrrh and aloes with all the chief ointments.

text.NRSV: a garden fountain, a well of living water, and flowing streams from Lebanon.
text.Rotherham: A garden fountain, a well of living waters,—and flowings from Lebanon.
text.KJV: 4:15 A fountaine of gardens, a well of liuing waters, and streames from Lebanon.
text.NABRE: A garden fountain, a well of living water, streams flowing from Lebanon.
text.DRB: The fountain of gardens: the well of living waters, which run with violence from Libanus.

text.NRSV: Awake, O north wind, and come, O south wind! Blow upon my garden that its fragrance may be wafted abroad. Let my beloved come to his garden, and eat its choicest fruits.
text.Rotherham: [SHE] Awake, O north wind, and come in, thou south, Fan my garden—its balsams, will flow out,—Let my beloved enter his garden, and eat his precious fruits.
text.KJV: 4:16 ¶ Awake, O Northwinde, and come thou South, blow vpon my garden, that the spices thereof may flow out: let my beloued come into his garden, and eate his pleasant fruits.
text.NABRE: Awake, north wind! Come, south wind! Blow upon my garden that its perfumes may spread abroad. W Let my lover come to his garden and eat its fruits of choicest yield.
text.DRB: Arise Northwind, & come Southwind, blow through my garden, and let the aromatical spices thereof flow.

text.NRSV: I come to my garden, my sister, my bride; I gather my myrrh with my spice, I eat my honeycomb with my honey, I drink my wine with my milk. Eat, friends, drink, and be drunk with love.
text.Rotherham: [HE] I have entered my garden, my sister, bride, I have plucked my myrrh, with my balsam, I have eaten the honey of my thicket, I have drunk my wine, with my milk:—Eat ye, O friends, Drink, yea drink abundantly, ye beloved! [****]
text.KJV: 5:1 I am come into my garden, my sister, my spouse, I haue gathered my Myrrhe with my spice, I haue eaten my honie combe with my hony, I haue drunke my wine with my milke: eate, O friends, drinke, yea drinke abundantly, O beloued!
text.NABRE: Chapter 5 M I have come to my garden, my sister, my bride; I gather my myrrh with my spices, I eat my honeycomb with my honey, I drink my wine with my milk. D? Eat, friends; drink! Drink deeply, lovers!
text.DRB: Let my beloved come into his garden, and eat the fruit of his apple-trees. I am come into my garden o my sister spouse, I have reaped my myrrh, with mine aromatical spices: I have eaten the honey-comb with mine honey, I have drunk my wine with my milk: eat o friends, and drink, and be inebriated my dearest.

text.NRSV: I slept, but my heart was awake. Listen! my beloved is knocking. “Open to me, my sister, my love, my dove, my perfect one; for my head is wet with dew, my locks with the drops of the night.”
text.Rotherham: [SHE] I, was sleeping, but, my heart, was awake,—The voice of my beloved—knocking! Open to me, my sister, my fair one, my dove, my perfect one, for, my head, is filled with dew, my locks, with the moisture of the night.
text.KJV: 5:2 ¶ I sleepe, but my heart waketh: it is the voyce of my beloued that knocketh, saying, Open to me, my sister, my loue, my doue, my vndefiled: for my head is filled with dewe, and my lockes with the drops of the night.
text.NABRE: A Fruitless Search W I was sleeping, but my heart was awake. The sound of my lover knocking! “Open to me, my sister, my friend, my dove, my perfect one! For my head is wet with dew, my hair, with the moisture of the night.”
text.DRB: I sleep, and my heart watcheth: the voice of my beloved knocking: Open to me my sister, my love, my dove, mine immaculate: because my head is full of dew, and my locks of the drops of the nights.

text.NRSV: I had put off my garment; how could I put it on again? I had washed my feet; how could I sully them?
text.Rotherham: I have put off my tunic, oh how shall I put it on? I have bathed my feet, oh how shall I soil them?
text.KJV: 5:3 I haue put off my coate, how shall I put it on? I haue washed my feete, how shall I defile them?
text.NABRE: I have taken off my robe, am I then to put it on? I have bathed my feet, am I then to soil them?
text.DRB: I have spoiled myself of my robe, how shall I be clothed with it? I have washed my feet, how shall I defile them?

text.NRSV: My beloved thrust his hand into the opening, and my inmost being yearned for him.
text.Rotherham: My beloved, thrust in his hand, at the window, and, my feelings, were deeply moved for him:
text.KJV: 5:4 My beloued put in his hand by the hole of the dore, and my bowels were moued for him.
text.NABRE: My lover put his hand in through the opening: my innermost being trembled because of him.
text.DRB: My beloved put his hand through the hole, and my belly trembled at his touch.

text.NRSV: I arose to open to my beloved, and my hands dripped with myrrh, my fingers with liquid myrrh, upon the handles of the bolt.
text.Rotherham: I myself, arose, to open to my beloved,—and, my hands, dripped with myrrh, and, my fingers, with myrrh distilling, upon the handles of the bolt.
text.KJV: 5:5 I rose vp to open to my beloued, and my hands dropped with myrrhe, and my fingers with sweete smelling myrrhe, vpon the handles of the locke.
text.NABRE: I rose to open for my lover, my hands dripping myrrh: My fingers, flowing myrrh upon the handles of the lock.
text.DRB: I arose, that I might open to my beloved: my hands have distilled myrrh, and my fingers are full of most approved myrrh.

text.NRSV: I opened to my beloved, but my beloved had turned and was gone. My soul failed me when he spoke. I sought him, but did not find him; I called him, but he gave no answer.
text.Rotherham: I myself, opened to my beloved, but, my beloved, had turned away, had passed on,—My soul, had gone out when he spake, I sought him, but found him not, I called him, but he answered not.
text.KJV: 5:6 I opened to my beloued, but my beloued had with drawen himselfe, and was gone: my soule failed when hee spake: I sought him, but I could not find him: I called him, but he gaue me no answere.
text.NABRE: I opened for my lover— but my lover had turned and gone! At his leaving, my soul sank. I sought him, but I did not find him; I called out after him, but he did not answer me.
text.DRB: I opened the bolt of my door to my beloved: but he had turned aside, and was passed. My soul melted, as he spake: I sought, and found him not: I called, and he did not answer me.

text.NRSV: The sentinels found me as they went about in the city; they beat me, they wounded me, they took away my mantle, those sentinels of the walls.
text.Rotherham: The watchmen who were going round in the city, found me, they smote me, wounded me,—The watchmen of the walls, took away my cloak from off me.
text.KJV: 5:7 The watchmen that went about the citie, found me, they smote me, they wounded me, the keepers of the walles tooke away my vaile from me.
text.NABRE: The watchmen found me, as they made their rounds in the city; They beat me, they wounded me, they tore off my mantle, the watchmen of the walls.
text.DRB: The keepers that go about the city found me: they struck me, and wounded me: the keepers of the walls took away my cloak.

text.NRSV: I adjure you, O daughters of Jerusalem, if you find my beloved, tell him this: I am faint with love.
text.Rotherham: I adjure you, ye daughters of Jerusalem,—If ye find my beloved, what will ye tell him? That, sick with love, I am.
text.KJV: 5:8 I charge you, O daughters of Ierusalem, if ye find my beloued, that yee tell him, that I am sicke of loue.
text.NABRE: I adjure you, Daughters of Jerusalem, if you find my lover What shall you tell him? that I am sick with love.
text.DRB: I adjure you o daughters of Jerusalem, if you shall find my beloved, that you tell him that I languish with love.

text.NRSV: What is your beloved more than another beloved, O fairest among women? What is your beloved more than another beloved, that you thus adjure us?
text.Rotherham: [DAUGHTERS OF JERUSALEM] What is thy beloved more than any other beloved, thou most beautiful among women? What is thy beloved more than any other beloved, that, thus, thou hast adjured us?
text.KJV: 5:9 ¶ What is thy beloued more then another beloued, O thou fairest among women? what is thy beloued more then another beloued, that thou doest so charge vs?
text.NABRE: The Lost Lover Described D How does your lover differ from any other lover, most beautiful among women? How does your lover differ from any other, that you adjure us so?
text.DRB: What manner of one is thy beloved of the beloved, o most beautiful of women? What manner of one is thy beloved of the beloved, that thou hast so adjured us?

text.NRSV: My beloved is all radiant and ruddy, distinguished among ten thousand.
text.Rotherham: [SHE.] My beloved, is white and ruddy, conspicuous beyond ten thousand:
text.KJV: 5:10 My beloued is white and ruddy, the chiefest among tenne thousand.
text.NABRE: W My lover is radiant and ruddy; outstanding among thousands.
text.DRB: My beloved is white and ruddy, chosen of thousands.

text.NRSV: His head is the finest gold; his locks are wavy, black as a raven.
text.Rotherham: His head, is pure gold,—his locks, are bushy, black as a raven;
text.KJV: 5:11 His head is as the most fine gold, his locks are bushy, and blacke as a Rauen.
text.NABRE: His head is gold, pure gold, his hair like palm fronds, as black as a raven.
text.DRB: His head is as the best gold: his hairs as the branches of palm-trees, black as a raven.

text.NRSV: His eyes are like doves beside springs of water, bathed in milk, fitly set.
text.Rotherham: His eyes, like doves, by the channels of water,—bathing in milk, set as gems in a ring:
text.KJV: 5:12 His eyes are as the eyes of doues by the riuers of water, washed with milk, and fitly set.
text.NABRE: His eyes are like doves beside streams of water, Bathing in milk, sitting by brimming pools.
text.DRB: His eyes as doves upon the little rivers of waters, which are washed with milk, and sit beside the most full streams.

text.NRSV: His cheeks are like beds of spices, yielding fragrance. His lips are lilies, distilling liquid myrrh.
text.Rotherham: His cheeks, like a raised bed of balsam, growing plants of perfume,—His lips, lilies, dripping with myrrh distilling:
text.KJV: 5:13 His cheekes are as a bed of spices, as sweete flowers: his lippes like lillies, dropping sweete smelling myrrhe.
text.NABRE: His cheeks are like beds of spices yielding aromatic scents; his lips are lilies that drip flowing myrrh.
text.DRB: His cheeks are as little beds of aromatical spices set of the pigmentaries. His lips are as lilies distilling principal myrrh.

text.NRSV: His arms are rounded gold, set with beryl. His body is ivory work, encrusted with sapphires.
text.Rotherham: His hands, cylinders of gold, set with topaz,—His body, wrought work of ivory, covered with sapphires:
text.KJV: 5:14 His hands are as gold rings set with the Berill: His belly is as bright iuorie, ouerlayd with Saphires.
text.NABRE: His arms are rods of gold adorned with gems; His loins, a work of ivory covered with sapphires.
text.DRB: His hands wrought round of gold, full of hyacinths. His belly of ivory, distinguished with sapphires.

text.NRSV: His legs are phoenix-pillars of alabaster, set on bases of gold. His appearance is like Lebanon, choice as the cedars.
text.Rotherham: His legs, pillars of white marble, founded on sockets of gold,—His form, like Lebanon, choice as cedars:
text.KJV: 5:15 His legges are as pillars of marble, set vpon sockets of fine gold: his countenance is as Lebanon, excellent as the Cedars.
text.NABRE: His legs, pillars of alabaster, resting on golden pedestals. His appearance, like the Lebanon, imposing as the cedars.
text.DRB: His thighs as pillars of marble, that are upon feet of gold. His form as of Libanus, elect as the cedars.

text.NRSV: His speech is most sweet, and he is altogether desirable. This is my beloved and this is my friend, O daughters of Jerusalem.
text.Rotherham: His mouth, most sweet, yea, altogether, he is delightful,—This, is my beloved, yea, this, is my dear one, ye daughters of Jerusalem.
text.KJV: 5:16 His mouth is most sweete, yea he is altogether louely. This is my beloued, and this is my friend, O daughters of Ierusalem.
text.NABRE: His mouth is sweetness itself; he is delightful in every way. Such is my lover, and such my friend, Daughters of Jerusalem!
text.DRB: His throat most sweet, and he whole to be desired: such an one is my beloved, and he is my friend, o daughters of Jerusalem.

text.NRSV: Where has your beloved gone, O fairest among women? Which way has your beloved turned, that we may seek him with you?
text.Rotherham: [DAUGHTERS OF JERUSALEM] Whither hath thy beloved, gone, thou most beautiful among women? whither hath thy beloved turned him aside? That we may seek him with thee.
text.KJV: 6:1 Whither is thy beloued gone? O thou fairest among women, whither is thy beloued turned aside? that we may seeke him with thee.
text.NABRE: Chapter 6 The Lost Lover Found D Where has your lover gone, most beautiful among women? Where has your lover withdrawn that we may seek him with you?
text.DRB: Whither is thy beloved gone o most beautiful of women? Whither is thy beloved turned aside? And we will seek him with thee.

text.NRSV: My beloved has gone down to his garden, to the beds of spices, to pasture his flock in the gardens, and to gather lilies.
text.Rotherham: [SHE] My beloved, is gone down to his garden, to the beds of balsam,—to pasture in the gardens, and to gather lilies,
text.KJV: 6:2 My beloued is gone downe into his garden, to the beds of spices, to feede in the gardens, and to gather lillies.
text.NABRE: W My lover has come down to his garden, to the beds of spices, To feed in the gardens and to gather lilies.
text.DRB: My beloved is gone down into the garden, to the bed of aromatical spices, to feed in the gardens, and to gather lilies.

text.NRSV: I am my beloved’s and my beloved is mine; he pastures his flock among the lilies.
text.Rotherham: I, am, my beloved’s, and, my beloved, is mine, he that pastureth among lilies.
text.KJV: 6:3 I am my beloueds, & my beloued is mine: he feedeth among the lillies.
text.NABRE: I belong to my lover, and my lover belongs to me; he feeds among the lilies.
text.DRB: Ito my beloved, and my beloved to me, who feedeth among the lilies.

text.NRSV: You are beautiful as Tirzah, my love, comely as Jerusalem, terrible as an army with banners.
text.Rotherham: [HE] Beautiful, art thou, my fair one, as Tirzah, comely, as Jerusalem,—majestic as bannered hosts!
text.KJV: 6:4 ¶ Thou art beautifull, O my loue, as Tirzah, comely as Ierusalem, terrible as an armie with banners.
text.NABRE: The Beauty of the Woman M Beautiful as Tirzah are you, my friend; fair as Jerusalem, fearsome as celestial visions!
text.DRB: Thou art fair o my love, sweet, and comely as Jerusalem: terrible as the army of a camp set in array.

text.NRSV: Turn away your eyes from me, for they overwhelm me! Your hair is like a flock of goats, moving down the slopes of Gilead.
text.Rotherham: Turn away thine eyes from me, for, they, have excited me,—Thy hair, is like a flock of goats, that are reclining on the sides of Mount Gilead:
text.KJV: 6:5 Turne away thine eyes from me, for they haue ouercome me: thy haire is a flocke of goates, that appeare from Gilead.
text.NABRE: Turn your eyes away from me, for they stir me up. Your hair is like a flock of goats streaming down from Gilead.
text.DRB: Turn away thine eyes from me, because they have made me fly away. Thy hairs as a flock of goats, which have appeared from Galaad.

text.NRSV: Your teeth are like a flock of ewes, that have come up from the washing; all of them bear twins, and not one among them is bereaved.
text.Rotherham: Thy teeth, are like a flock of sheep which have come up from the washing-place,—whereof, all of them, are twin-bearers, and bereaved, is there none among them:
text.KJV: 6:6 Thy teeth are as a flocke of sheepe which goe vp from the washing, wherof euery one beareth twinnes, and there is not one barren among them.
text.NABRE: Your teeth are like a flock of ewes that come up from the washing, All of them big with twins, none of them barren.
text.DRB: Thy teeth as a flock of sheep, which have come up from the lavatory, all with twins, and there is no barren among them.

text.NRSV: Your cheeks are like halves of a pomegranate behind your veil.
text.Rotherham: Like a slice of pomegranate, are thy temples, from behind thy veil:
text.KJV: 6:7 As a piece of a pomegranat are thy temples within thy lockes.
text.NABRE: Like pomegranate halves, your cheeks behind your veil.
text.DRB: As the bark of a pomegranate, so are thy cheeks, beside thy hidden.

text.NRSV: There are sixty queens and eighty concubines, and maidens without number.
text.Rotherham: Threescore, are the queens, and, fourscore, are the concubines,—and, virgins, there are, without number.
text.KJV: 6:8 There are threescore Queenes, and fourescore concubines, and virgins without number.
text.NABRE: Sixty are the queens, eighty the concubines, and young women without number—
text.DRB: There are threescore queens, & fourscore concubines, and of young maids there is no number.

text.NRSV: My dove, my perfect one, is the only one, the darling of her mother, flawless to her that bore her. The maidens saw her and called her happy; the queens and concubines also, and they praised her.
text.Rotherham: One alone, is my dove, my perfect one, one alone, was she to her mother, Pure, was she to her that bare her,—The daughters, have seen her, and pronounced her happy, Queens and concubines, and they have praised her. [****]
text.KJV: 6:9 My doue, my vndefiled is but one; she is the only one of her mother, she is the choice one of her that bare her: The daughters sawe her, and blessed her; yea the Queenes and the concubins, and they praysed her.
text.NABRE: One alone is my dove, my perfect one, her mother’s special one, favorite of the one who bore her. Daughters see her and call her happy, queens and concubines, and they praise her:
text.DRB: My dove is one, my perfect one, she is the only of her mother, elect to her that bare her. The daughters have seen her, and declared her to be most blessed: the queens and concubines, and have praised her.

text.NRSV: “Who is this that looks forth like the dawn, fair as the moon, bright as the sun, terrible as an army with banners?”
text.Rotherham: [THEY] Who is this, that looketh forth like the dawn, beautiful as the moon, pure as the sun, majestic as bannered hosts?
text.KJV: 6:10 ¶ Who is she that looketh forth as the morning, faire as the moone, cleare as the sunne, and terrible as an armie with banners?
text.NABRE: “Who is this that comes forth like the dawn, beautiful as the white moon, pure as the blazing sun, fearsome as celestial visions?”
text.DRB: What is she, that cometh forth as the morning rising, fair as the moon, elect as the sun, terrible as the army of a camp set in array?

text.NRSV: I went down to the nut orchard, to look at the blossoms of the valley, to see whether the vines had budded, whether the pomegranates were in bloom.
text.Rotherham: [HE] To the garden of nuts, I went down, to look at the fresh shoots of the ravine,—to see whether: had burst forth the vine, had blossomed the pomegranate:—
text.KJV: 6:11 I went downe into the garden of nuts to see the fruits of the valley, and to see whether the vine flourished, and the pomegranats budded.
text.NABRE: Love’s Meeting W To the walnut grove I went down, to see the young growth of the valley; To see if the vines were in bloom, if the pomegranates had blossomed.
text.DRB: I came down into the garden of nuts, to see the fruits of the valleys, and to look if the vineyard had flourished, and the pomegranates budded.

text.NRSV: Before I was aware, my fancy set me in a chariot beside my prince.
text.Rotherham: I know not [how it was], my soul, set for me the chariots of my willing people!
text.KJV: 6:12 Or euer I was aware, my soule made me like the chariots of Amminadib.
text.NABRE: Before I knew it, my desire had made me the blessed one of the prince’s people.
text.DRB: I knew not: my soul troubled me for the chariots of Aminadab.

text.NRSV: Return, return, O Shulammite! Return, return, that we may look upon you. Why should you look upon the Shulammite, as upon a dance before two armies?
text.Rotherham: [THEY] Return, return, O Shulamite, Return, return, that we may look on thee! [SHE] What would ye look on in the Shulamite? [THEY] As it were the dance of a double camp…
text.KJV: 6:13 Returne, returne, O Shulamite; returne, returne, that we may looke vpon thee: what will yee see in the Shulamite? as it were the company of two armies.
text.NABRE: Turn, turn, O Shulammite! turn, turn that we may gaze upon you! How can you gaze upon the Shulammite as at the dance of the two camps?
text.DRB: Return, return o Sulamitess: return, return, that we may behold thee.

text.NRSV: How graceful are your feet in sandals, O queenly maiden! Your rounded thighs are like jewels, the work of a master hand.
text.Rotherham: How beautiful, are thy feet in sandals, O daughter of a noble,—The curvings of thy hips, are like ornaments wrought by the hands of a skilled workman:
text.KJV: 7:1 Howe beautifull are thy feete with shooes, O princes daughter! the ioynts of thy thighs are like iewels, the worke of the hands of a cunning workman.
text.NABRE: Chapter 7 The Beauty of the Beloved D? Turn, turn, O Shulammite! turn, turn that we may gaze upon you! W How can you gaze upon the Shulammite as at the dance of the two camps?
text.DRB: What shalt thou see in the Sulamitess but the companies of camps? How beautiful are thy passes in shoes, o prince's daughter! The joints of thy thighs are as jewels, that are made by the hand of the artificer.

text.NRSV: Your navel is a rounded bowl that never lacks mixed wine. Your belly is a heap of wheat, encircled with lilies.
text.Rotherham: Thy navel, is a round bowl, may it not lack spiced wine! Thy body, a heap of wheat fenced about with lilies;
text.KJV: 7:2 Thy nauell is like a round goblet, which wanteth not licour: thy belly is like an heape of wheate, set about with lillies.
text.NABRE: M How beautiful are your feet in sandals, O noble daughter! Your curving thighs like jewels, the product of skilled hands.
text.DRB: Thy navel as a round bowl, never wanting cups. Thy belly as an heap of wheat, compassed about with lilies.

text.NRSV: Your two breasts are like two fawns, twins of a gazelle.
text.Rotherham: Thy two breasts, are like two young roes, the twins of a gazelle:
text.KJV: 7:3 Thy two breasts are like two yong Roes that are twinnes.
text.NABRE: Your valley, a round bowl that should never lack mixed wine. Your belly, a mound of wheat, encircled with lilies.
text.DRB: Thy two breasts, as two fawns the twins of a roe.

text.NRSV: Your neck is like an ivory tower. Your eyes are pools in Heshbon, by the gate of Bath-rabbim. Your nose is like a tower of Lebanon, overlooking Damascus.
text.Rotherham: Thy neck, is like a tower of ivory,—Thine eyes, are pools in Heshbon, by the gate of Bath-rabbim, Thy nose, is like the tower of Lebanon, which looketh towards Damascus:
text.KJV: 7:4 Thy necke is as a towre of yuory: thine eyes like the fish pooles in Heshbon, by the gate of Bathrabbim: thy nose is as the towre of Lebanon, which looketh toward Damascus.
text.NABRE: Your breasts are like two fawns, twins of a gazelle.
text.DRB: Thy neck as a tower of ivory. Thine eyes as the fish-pools in Hesebon, which are in the gate of the daughter of the multitude. Thy nose as the tower of Libanus, that looketh against Damascus.

text.NRSV: Your head crowns you like Carmel, and your flowing locks are like purple; a king is held captive in the tresses.
text.Rotherham: Thy head upon thee, is like Carmel, And, the hair of thy head, is like purple,—The king, is held captive by the ringlets!
text.KJV: 7:5 Thine head vpon thee is like Carmel, and the haire of thine head like purple, the king is held in the galleries.
text.NABRE: Your neck like a tower of ivory; your eyes, pools in Heshbon by the gate of Bath-rabbim. Your nose like the tower of Lebanon that looks toward Damascus.
text.DRB: Thy head as Carmelus: and the hairs of thy head as a king's purple tied to conduit pipes.

text.NRSV: How fair and pleasant you are, O loved one, delectable maiden!
text.Rotherham: [HE] How beautiful, and how delightful, O dear love, for delights:
text.KJV: 7:6 How faire, and how pleasant art thou, O Loue, for delights!
text.NABRE: Your head rises upon you like Carmel; your hair is like purple; a king is caught in its locks.
text.DRB: How beautiful art thou, and how comely my dearest, in delights!

text.NRSV: You are stately as a palm tree, and your breasts are like its clusters.
text.Rotherham: This thy stature, is like to a palm-tree, and, thy breasts, are like clusters:
text.KJV: 7:7 This thy stature is like to a palme tree, and thy breasts to clusters of grapes.
text.NABRE: Love’s Desires How beautiful you are, how fair, my love, daughter of delights!
text.DRB: Thy stature is like to a palm-tree, & thy breasts to clusters of grapes.

text.NRSV: I say I will climb the palm tree and lay hold of its branches. O may your breasts be like clusters of the vine, and the scent of your breath like apples,
text.Rotherham: I said, I will ascend the palm-tree, I will lay hold of its fruit stalks—Oh then, let thy breasts, I pray thee, be like vine-clusters, And, the fragrance of thy nose, like apples;
text.KJV: 7:8 I said, I will goe vp to the palme tree, I will take hold of the boughes thereof: now also thy breasts shall be as clusters of the vine, and the smell of thy nose, like apples.
text.NABRE: Your very form resembles a date-palm, and your breasts, clusters.
text.DRB: Isaid: I will go up into the palm-tree, and will take hold of the fruits thereof: and thy breasts shall be as the clusters of a vineyard: and the odour of thy mouth as it were of apples.

text.NRSV: and your kisses like the best wine that goes down smoothly, gliding over lips and teeth.
text.Rotherham: And, thy mouth, like good wine—[SHE] Flowing to my beloved smoothly, gliding over the lips of the sleeping.
text.KJV: 7:9 And the roofe of thy mouth like the best wine, for my beloued, that goeth downe sweetely, causing the lippes of those that are asleepe, to speake.
text.NABRE: I thought, “Let me climb the date-palm! Let me take hold of its branches! Let your breasts be like clusters of the vine and the fragrance of your breath like apples,
text.DRB: Thy throat as the best wine, worthy for my beloved to drink, & for his lips and his teeth to ruminate.

text.NRSV: I am my beloved’s, and his desire is for me.
text.Rotherham: I, am my beloved’s, and, unto me, is his longing.
text.KJV: 7:10 ¶ I am my beloueds, and his desire is towards me.
text.NABRE: And your mouth like the best wine— W that flows down smoothly for my lover, gliding over my lips and teeth.
text.DRB: Ito my beloved, and his turning is toward me.

text.NRSV: Come, my beloved, let us go forth into the fields, and lodge in the villages;
text.Rotherham: Come, my beloved, Let us go forth into the country, Let us stay the night in the villages:
text.KJV: 7:11 Come, my beloued, let vs goe forth into the field: let vs lodge in the villages.
text.NABRE: I belong to my lover, his yearning is for me.
text.DRB: Come my beloved, let us go forth into the field, let us abide in the villages.

text.NRSV: let us go out early to the vineyards, and see whether the vines have budded, whether the grape blossoms have opened and the pomegranates are in bloom. There I will give you my love.
text.Rotherham: Let us get up early to the vineyards, Let us see whether the vine, hath burst forth, the blossom, hath opened, the pomegranates, have bloomed,—There, will I give my caresses to thee.
text.KJV: 7:12 Let vs get vp earely to the vineyards, let vs see if the vine flourish, whether the tender grape appeare, and the pomegranates bud forth: there will I giue thee my loues.
text.NABRE: Come, my lover! Let us go out to the fields, let us pass the night among the henna.
text.DRB: Let us rise early to the vineyards, let us see if the vineyard flourish, if the flowers be ready to bring forth fruits, if the pomegranates flourish: there will I give thee my breasts.

text.NRSV: The mandrakes give forth fragrance, and over our doors are all choice fruits, new and old, which I have laid up for you, O my beloved.
text.Rotherham: The love-apples, have given fragrance, and, at our openings, are all precious things, new and yet old,—O my beloved! I have treasured them up for thee.
text.KJV: 7:13 The mandrakes giue a smell, and at our gates are all maner of pleasant fruits, new and olde, which I haue laid vp for thee, O my beloued.
text.NABRE: Let us go early to the vineyards, and see if the vines are in bloom, If the buds have opened, if the pomegranates have blossomed; There will I give you my love.
text.DRB: The Mandragoræs have given a smell. In our gates all fruits: the new and the old, my beloved, I have kept for thee.

text.NRSV: O that you were like a brother to me, who nursed at my mother’s breast! If I met you outside, I would kiss you, and no one would despise me.
text.Rotherham: Oh that thou hadst been a very brother to me, who had sucked the breasts of my own mother,—Had I found thee without, I had kissed thee, Yea, folk would not have despised me!
text.KJV: 8:1 O that thou wert as my brother that sucked the brests of my mother, when I should find thee without, I would kisse thee, yet I should not be despised.
text.NABRE: Chapter 8 Would that you were a brother to me, nursed at my mother’s breasts! If I met you out of doors, I would kiss you and none would despise me.
text.DRB: Who shall give to me thee my brother, sucking the breasts of my mother, that I may find thee without, and kiss thee, and now no man despise me?

text.NRSV: I would lead you and bring you into the house of my mother, and into the chamber of her who conceived me. I would give you spiced wine to drink, the juice of my pomegranates.
text.Rotherham: I would have guided thee—brought thee into the house of my mother, Thou wouldst have instructed me,—I would have let thee drink of spiced wine, of the pressed-out juice of my pomegranate.
text.KJV: 8:2 I would leade thee, and bring thee into my mothers house, who would instruct me: I would cause thee to drinke of spiced wine, of the iuice of my pomegranate.
text.NABRE: I would lead you, bring you to my mother’s house, where you would teach me, Where I would give you to drink spiced wine, my pomegranate juice.
text.DRB: I will take hold of thee, and will bring thee into my mother's house: there thou shalt teach me, and I will give thee a cup of spiced wine, and new wine of my pomegranates.

text.NRSV: O that his left hand were under my head, and that his right hand embraced me!
text.Rotherham: His left hand under my head, then, his right hand, embraceth me.
text.KJV: 8:3 His left hand should be vnder my head, and his right hand should embrace me.
text.NABRE: His left hand is under my head, and his right arm embraces me.
text.DRB: His left hand under my head, and his right hand shall embrace me.

text.NRSV: I adjure you, O daughters of Jerusalem, do not stir up or awaken love until it is ready.
text.Rotherham: I [HE] adjure you, O ye daughters of Jerusalem,—Why will ye wake, and why will ye arouse the dear love until she please! [****]
text.KJV: 8:4 I charge you, O daughters of Ierusalem, that ye stirre not vp, nor awake my loue vntill he please.
text.NABRE: I adjure you, Daughters of Jerusalem, do not awaken or stir up love until it is ready!
text.DRB: I adjure you o daughters of Jerusalem, that you raise not up, nor make the beloved to awake till herself will.

text.NRSV: Who is that coming up from the wilderness, leaning upon her beloved? Under the apple tree I awakened you. There your mother was in labor with you; there she who bore you was in labor.
text.Rotherham: [THEY] Who is this, coming up out of the wilderness, leaning upon her beloved? [HE] Under the apple-tree, I roused thee, where thy mother, was in pain with thee, where she was in pain who gave thee birth!
text.KJV: 8:5 (Who is this that commeth vp from the wildernesse, leaning vpon her beloued?) I raised thee vp vnder the apple tree: there thy mother brought thee forth, there she brought thee forth, that bare thee.
text.NABRE: The Return from the Desert D? Who is this coming up from the desert, leaning upon her lover? W Beneath the apple tree I awakened you; there your mother conceived you; there she who bore you conceived.
text.DRB: Who is this, that cometh up from the desert, flowing with delights, leaning upon her beloved? Under the apple-tree I raised thee up: there thy mother was corrupted, there she was deflowered that bare thee.

text.NRSV: Set me as a seal upon your heart, as a seal upon your arm; for love is strong as death, passion fierce as the grave. Its flashes are flashes of fire, a raging flame.
text.Rotherham: [SHE] Set me as a seal, upon thy heart, as a seal upon thine arm, For, mighty as death, is love, Exacting as hades, is jealousy,—The flames thereof, are flames of fire, The flash of Yah!
text.KJV: 8:6 ¶ Set mee as a seale vpon thine heart, as a seale vpon thine arme: for loue is strong as death, iealousie is cruel as the graue: the coales thereof are coales of fire, which hath a most vehement flame.
text.NABRE: True Love Set me as a seal upon your heart, as a seal upon your arm; For Love is strong as Death, longing is fierce as Sheol. Its arrows are arrows of fire, flames of the divine.
text.DRB: Put me as a seal upon thy heart, as a seal upon thine arm: because love is strong as death: jealousy is hard as hell, the lamps thereof lamps of fire and flames.

text.NRSV: Many waters cannot quench love, neither can floods drown it. If one offered for love all the wealth of one’s house, it would be utterly scorned.
text.Rotherham: Many waters, cannot quench love, nor shall, floods, overwhelm it,—If a man would give all the substance of his house, for love, they would, utterly despise, him. [****]
text.KJV: 8:7 Many waters cannot quench loue, neither can the floods drowne it: if a man would giue all the substance of his house for loue, it would vtterly be contemned.
text.NABRE: Deep waters cannot quench love, nor rivers sweep it away. Were one to offer all the wealth of his house for love, he would be utterly despised.
text.DRB: Many waters cannot quench charity, neither shall floods overwhelm it: if a man shall give all the substance of his house for love, as nothing he shall despise it.

text.NRSV: We have a little sister, and she has no breasts. What shall we do for our sister, on the day when she is spoken for?
text.Rotherham: [THEY] A sister, have we, a little one, and, breasts, hath she none, What shall we do for our sister, in the day when she may be spoken for?
text.KJV: 8:8 ¶ We haue a litle sister, and shee hath no breasts: what shall we doe for our sister, in the day when she shall bee spoken for?
text.NABRE: An Answer to the Brothers W “We have a little sister; she has no breasts as yet. What shall we do for our sister on the day she is spoken for?
text.DRB: Our sister is little, and hath no breasts. What shall we do to our sister in the day when she is to be spoken unto?

text.NRSV: If she is a wall, we will build upon her a battlement of silver; but if she is a door, we will enclose her with boards of cedar.
text.Rotherham: If, a wall, she is, we will build upon it a battlement of silver,—but if, a door, she is, we will close it up with a plank of cedar.
text.KJV: 8:9 If she be a wall, we will build vpon her a palace of siluer: and if she bee a dore, we will inclose her with boards of Cedar.
text.NABRE: If she is a wall, we will build upon her a silver turret; But if she is a door, we will board her up with cedar planks.”
text.DRB: If she be a wall, let us build upon it bulwarks of silver: if she be a door, let us join it together with boards of cedar.

text.NRSV: I was a wall, and my breasts were like towers; then I was in his eyes as one who brings peace.
text.Rotherham: [SHE] I, was a wall, and, my breasts, like towers,—Then, became I, in his eyes, one who did indeed find good content.
text.KJV: 8:10 I am a wall, and my breasts like towers: then was I in his eyes as one that found fauour.
text.NABRE: I am a wall, and my breasts are like towers. I became in his eyes as one who brings peace.
text.DRB: I am a wall: and my breasts are as a tower, since I was made before him as one finding peace.

text.NRSV: Solomon had a vineyard at Baal-hamon; he entrusted the vineyard to keepers; each one was to bring for its fruit a thousand pieces of silver.
text.Rotherham: A vineyard, had Solomon, as the owner of abundance, He put out the vineyard to keepers,—Every man, was to bring in, for the fruit thereof, a thousand silverlings:
text.KJV: 8:11 Solomon had a vineyard at Baalhamon, hee let out the vineyard vnto keepers: euery one for the fruit thereof was to bring a thousand pieces of siluer.
text.NABRE: A Boast M? Solomon had a vineyard at Baal-hamon; he gave over the vineyard to caretakers. For its fruit one would have to pay a thousand silver pieces.
text.DRB: The peace-maker had a vineyard, in that which hath peoples: he delivered the same to keepers, a man bringeth for the fruit thereof a thousand pieces of silver.

text.NRSV: My vineyard, my very own, is for myself; you, O Solomon, may have the thousand, and the keepers of the fruit two hundred!
text.Rotherham: Mine own vineyard, is before me,—The thousand belong to thee, O Solomon, and two hundred to the keepers of the fruit thereof.
text.KJV: 8:12 My vineyard which is mine, is before me: thou (O Solomon) must haue a thousand, and those that keepe the fruit thereof, two hundred.
text.NABRE: My vineyard is at my own disposal; the thousand pieces are for you, Solomon, and two hundred for the caretakers of its fruit.
text.DRB: My vineyard is before me. A thousand are thy peace-makers, and two hundred for them, that keep the fruits thereof.

text.NRSV: O you who dwell in the gardens, my companions are listening for your voice; let me hear it.
text.Rotherham: [HE] O thou fair dweller in the gardens, the companions are giving heed to thy voice, Let me hear it.
text.KJV: 8:13 Thou that dwellest in the gardens, the companions hearken to thy voice: cause me to heare it.
text.NABRE: The Lovers’ Yearnings M You who dwell in the gardens, my companions are listening for your voice— let me hear it!
text.DRB: Thou dwellest in the gardens, the friends do harken: make me hear thy voice.

text.NRSV: Make haste, my beloved, and be like a gazelle or a young stag on the mountains of spices.
text.Rotherham: [SHE] Come quickly, my beloved, and resemble thou a gazelle, or a young stag, upon the mountains of balsam-trees.
text.KJV: 8:14 ¶ Make haste, my beloued, and be thou like to a Roe, or to a yong Hart vpon the mountaines of spices.
text.NABRE: W Swiftly, my lover, be like a gazelle or a young stag upon the mountains of spices.
text.DRB: Fly, o my beloved, and be like to the roe, and to the fawn of harts upon the mountains of aromatical spices.
